query: are there any known prognostic factors other than Child-Pugh score in hepatorenal syndrome besides infections and gastrointestinal bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Varices -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although gastric varices and portal hypertension an incidental acute gastric variceal bleeding is lower than esophageal variceal bleeding, gastric varices have a high propensity to bleed severely. They are often associated with higher mortality than esophageal variceal bleeding. The overall prognosis is dependent on the severity of the underlying disease and commonly used prognostic model such as Child-Pugh score and Model for end-stage liver disease (MELD) score is useful to predict the severity of underlying liver disease and also are effective in estimating the risk of mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Compensated chronic liver disease (who has not developed significant complications) usually carries a better prognosis than decompensated liver cirrhosis. Decompensated liver cirrhosis patients (who have developed variceal bleeding, ascites, HCC, SBP, and hepatorenal syndrome) have a poor prognosis. Mean survival of about six months if Child-Pugh score of 12 or greater or MELD score of 21 or higher. There are various scoring systems used to assess the severity of chronic liver disease. 1. The Child-Pugh score uses ascites, bilirubin, albumin, PT, and encephalopathy. Class A (score 5-6): well-compensated disease. Class B (score 7-9): functional compromise. Class C (score 10-15): decompensated disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Varices -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic variceal ligation should be repeated every 1 to 4 weeks until varices are eradicated. If TIPS is done, repeat endoscopy to assess rebleeding. Endoscopic screening should be done in patients with known cirrhosis every two to three years and yearly in patients with decompensated cirrhosis. Patients with liver stiffness less than 20 kPa and with platelets greater than 150,000 can avoid endoscopic screening and may follow up by annual TE and platelet count. Prognosis In cirrhosis, one-year survival is 50% for those surviving two weeks following a variceal bleed. In-hospital mortality remains high related to the severity of underlying cirrhosis, ranging from 0% in Child A to 32% in Child C disease. Prognosis in noncirrhotic portal fibrosis is better than for cirrhotics. Complications

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: For upper GI bleeds, in-hospital mortality rates are approximately 10% based on observational studies. This rate holds steady up to 1-month post-hospitalization for GI bleed. Long-term follow-up of patients with UGIB shows that at three years after admission mortality rates from all causes approach 37%.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Failure(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Coagulopathy: The most common site of bleeding is from the upper gastrointestinal tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Prognosis -- Iatrogenic Ulcers
retrieved_docs.content: Factors determining the severity of thermal injury after pulmonary vein isolation depend on ablation energy, catheter characteristics, duration, and catheter-tissue contact force. The prognosis for post-variceal banding ulcer bleeding is poor, with mortality rates ranging from 22.3% to 23.8%. Poor prognostic indicators include high Model for End-Stage Liver Disease scores, emergent endoscopic band ligation, the presence of hepatocellular carcinoma, and active bleeding at the time of endoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Limited studies exist regarding the prognosis following GI bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic-Associated Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with AH are subdivided into mild-moderate AH or severe AH. Patients with an MDF greater than 32, MELD score greater than 20, ABIC score category C, or a Glasgow AH score of 9 predicts higher mortality with a diagnosis of severe AH. Patients with severe AH with or without hepatic encephalopathy are considered candidates for a short course of prednisolone (40 mg/day for 28 days). Prednisolone is preferred to prednisone as it does not require metabolism in the liver for its therapeutic efficacy. For patients unable to take it orally, methylprednisolone, 32 mg intravenously daily, is an option. However, failure to respond to steroids within a week evident by a Lille score of greater than 0.45 indicates a lack of response to steroids which should be discontinued, thereafter. For patients with a Lille score of less than 0.45 (Lille responders), prednisolone should be continued for another three weeks. Glucocorticoids alter the expression of anti-inflammatory genes, thus promoting its anti-inflammatory role. Contraindications to steroid use include any active gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding, severe pancreatitis, uncontrolled diabetes, active infection, or renal failure. Such patients may be managed with pentoxifylline (400 mg orally, three times a day for 28 days). Hepatorenal syndrome is one of the major causes of death in patients with AH. Patients with acute kidney injury or hepatorenal syndrome respond poorly to corticosteroid therapy. Patients with bacterial infection may be treated with corticosteroids after the infection has been appropriately controlled with antibiotics. Response to prednisolone is graded as complete if Lille score is less than 0.16, partial if Lille score is between 0.16 and 0.56, or null if Lille score is greater than 0.56. A Lille score of more than 0.45 after 1 week of corticosteroid therapy is associated with 75% mortality at 6 months. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with alcoholic hepatitis subdivide into mild-moderate alcoholic hepatitis or severe alcoholic hepatitis. A DF (discriminant function) is calculated as 4.6 x (patient's prothrombin time - control prothrombin time) + total bilirubin of the patient. MELD score is calculated as a 3.8 x log (serum bilirubin [mg/dL]) + 11.2 x log (INR) + 9.6 x log (serum creatinine [mg/dL]) + 6.4.  For patients with MELD greater than 11, the score should be recalculated to account for serum sodium using the following equation: MELD(i) + 1.32 x (137 - Na) - [0.033 x MELD(i) x (137 - Na)], where MELD(i) is the initial MELD score calculated in step one. Patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis with or without hepatic encephalopathy are considered candidates for prednisolone (40 mg/day for 28 days). Prednisolone is preferred to prednisone as it does not require metabolism in the liver for its therapeutic efficacy. For patients unable to take it orally, methylprednisolone, 32 mg intravenously daily, is an option. However, failure to respond to steroids within a week, evident by a Lille score of greater than 0.45, suggests a lack of response to steroids, which should then get discontinued. For patients with a Lille score of less than 0.45 (Lille responders), prednisolone should continue for another three weeks. Glucocorticoids alter the expression of anti-inflammatory genes, thus promoting its anti-inflammatory role. Contraindications to steroid use include any active gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding, severe pancreatitis, uncontrolled diabetes, active infection, or renal failure. Such patients may receive pentoxifylline (400 mg orally, three times a day for 28 days). Hepatorenal syndrome is one of the leading causes of death in patients with alcoholic hepatitis. Patients with acute kidney injury or hepatorenal syndrome respond poorly to corticosteroid therapy. Patients with bacterial infection may be treated with corticosteroids after the infection is under control with antibiotics. Response to prednisolone is graded as complete if the Lille score is less than 0.16, partial if the Lille score is between 0.16 and 0.56), or null if the Lille score is greater than 0.56. A Lille score of more than 0.45 after one week of corticosteroid therapy is associated with 75% mortality at six months. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality rates were higher in women than in men when adjusted for age, which differs from that of lower GI bleeding. Patients with multiple hospitalizations for GI bleeding carry higher mortality rates. The long-term prognosis was worst in patients who suffered from malignancies and variceal bleeds. The prognosis was worse with advancing age. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, patients who experience SBP have chronic liver disease with a Child-Pugh classification, which assesses the prognosis of liver disease, of C. This ranking involves a high to a maximum score of 10 to 15 points (on the Child-Pugh scale) and measures 1-year patient survival at 45% and 2-year survival at 35%. Decompensated cirrhotic patients are those at the highest risk of developing SBP. Infecting organisms typically originate from the intestinal lumen, from where they pass via translocation to mesenteric lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal Hypertension -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of portal hypertension largely depends on the underlying etiology, severity of liver disease, and the presence of complications. Patients with CSPH, particularly those with an HVPG ≥10 mm Hg, face a higher risk of decompensation, including ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, and variceal bleeding. Once HVPG reaches ≥12 mm Hg, the likelihood of life-threatening bleeding and other complications increases significantly.

retrieved_docs.title: Use Of The Child Pugh Score In Liver Disease -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The Child-Pugh score has been validated as a predictor of postoperative mortality after portocaval shunt surgery and predicts mortality risk associated with other major operations. After abdominal surgery, Child class A patients have a 10% mortality rate; Child class B patients have a 30% mortality rate, and Child class C patients have a 70 to 80% mortality rate [5] [6] Child class A patients are generally considered safe candidates for elective surgery. Child class B patients can proceed with surgery after medical optimization but still have increased risk. Elective surgery is contraindicated in Child class C patients. The Child-Pugh score can help predict all-cause mortality risk and development of other complications from liver dysfunction, such as variceal bleeding, as well. In one study, overall mortality for these patients at one year was 0% for Child class A, 20% for Child class B, and 55% for Child class C. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Evaluation -- Risk Stratification
retrieved_docs.content: The Rockall score was designed to predict rebleeding and mortality and includes age, comorbidities, the presence of shock, and endoscopic stigmata. A pre-endoscopic Rockall scoring system is also available and can be used to stratify a patient's risk for rebleeding and mortality before the endoscopic evaluation. When the Rockall score is used, patients with ≤2 points are considered low risk and have a 4.3% probability of rebleeding and 0.1% mortality. In contrast, patients with a score of ≥6 have a rebleeding rate of 15% and mortality of 39%. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic therapy effectively achieves permanent control of UGIB in 80% to 90% of patients. However, 10% to 20% will rebleed. In a prospective randomized study of patients with rebleeding following initial endoscopic intervention of peptic ulcers, 73% of these patients achieved hemostasis with repeat endoscopy and avoided surgery. [70] The majority of patients with ulcer bleeding die from other diseases rather than from bleeding. In a cohort study of 10,428 patients presenting with peptic ulcer disease, 80% of the deaths were not due to bleeding but to underlying medical comorbidities. [38] Despite therapeutic advancements, patients presenting with acute variceal hemorrhage still have a 6-week mortality risk of 10% to 15%. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhage Control -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality ranged from 13% for Mallory-Weiss tear to 34% for gastritis & duodenitis, 38–41% for duodenal ulcer, gastric ulcer, and oesophagitis, 52% for varices, and 95% for upper GI malignancy. Mortality was also higher for bleeds that occurred as inpatients (54%) than for bleeds presenting at admission (36%). In the case of traumatic hemorrhage, prognosis depends on whether the hemorrhagic site is compressible or non-compressible, the time to medical treatment, and whether a tourniquet is applied or not.

retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is categorized as a form of upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and frequently presents with esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء). It is closely associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية), highlighting the clinical importance of identifying variceal manifestations when evaluating and managing patients with gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1169
retrieved_docs.content: Variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية) originates from dilated veins (وريد متدلي) and represents a specific cause of gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Gastrointestinal hemorrhage includes bleeding throughout the digestive tract, with upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نزيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) being a major subgroup. Consequently, variceal bleeding is a subtype of upper gastrointestinal bleeding and is closely associated with overall gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Recognizing that variceal bleeding stems from dilated veins aids clinicians in identifying the source of bleeding and optimizing management for patients experiencing gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1143
retrieved_docs.content: Varices van overige lokalisaties is a classification that includes دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف (esophageal varices without reported bleeding) and نزيف دَوالِي المريء (bleeding esophageal varices). تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic cirrhosis) is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء, indicating that patients with cirrhosis are at heightened risk for esophageal variceal bleeding. تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis) is a subtype of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, linking alcohol‑related liver disease to the broader condition of hepatic cirrhosis and its associated risk of bleeding esophageal varices. This interconnected knowledge underscores the importance of monitoring variceal status in cirrhotic patients, particularly those with an alcoholic etiology, to guide appropriate clinical management.
